coredata.dc:title: RecJf exonuclease-assisted fluorescent self-assembly aptasensor for supersensitive detection of pesticides in food
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Xia
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence quenching induced by exonuclease, specific target recognition by an aptamer, and host–guest recognition were artfully integrated in this study, and a new nanoscale fluorescent aptasensor for supersensitive recognition of acetamiprid was proposed. In the absence of acetamiprid, the aptamer hybridized with complementary DNA at 3′end-marked ferrocene (Fc) (cDNA-Fc) to form a double-stranded DNA. Fluorescence intensity was nearly unchanged after RecJf exonuclease was added. After the addition of acetamiprid, the aptamer preferentially bound with it to form a new compound; hence, the aptamer could not bind with cDNA-Fc. The single-stranded aptamer and cDNA-Fc were cut off after RecJf exonuclease was added, and the Fc released via host–guest recognition rapidly entered the cavity of cyclodextrin and initiated photoinduced electron transfer. Consequently, fluorescence intensity decreased, and acetamiprid was sensitively detected. The method can be extensively applied to detect various pesticides or additives in agriculture and forestry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pesticides
Acetamiprid
Aptamer
Photoinduced electron transfer
RecJf exonuclease

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity, interference, and stability of acetamiprid detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the designed nanosensors for acetamiprid detection via β-cyclodextrin host–guest recognition and the PET system.

Aptamer (lane 1), aptamer + RecJf exonuclease (lane 2), aptamer + acetamiprid (lane 3), aptamer + acetamiprid + RecJf exonuclease (lane 4), cDNA-Fc (lane 5), cDNA-Fc + RecJf exonuclease (lane 6), dsDNA (aptamer + cDNA-Fc) (lane 7), dsDNA (aptamer + cDNA-Fc) + RecJf exonuclease (lane 8), and dsDNA (aptamer + cDNA-Fc) + acetamiprid + RecJf exonuclease (lane 9).

Effect of fluorescence intensity induced by RecJf exonuclease. (a) β-CD-CDs, (b) β-CD-CDs + dsDNA (aptamer + cDNA-Fc) + RecJf exonuclease, (c) β-CD-CDs + aptamer-acetamiprid + cDNA-Fc, and (d) β-CD-CDs + aptamer-acetamiprid + cDNA-Fc + RecJf exonuclease.
(A) cDNA-Fc concentration, (B) dosage and (C) action time of RecJf exonuclease, and (D) time of Fe captured by β-CD-CDs. β-CD-CDs = 20 μg mL−1, acetamiprid = 1 μM.
Response curve between β-CD-CD (20 μg·mL−1) and fluorescence intensity at different acetamiprid concentrations.
Selectivity and anti-interference experiments on acetamiprid detection.

head.ce:title: RecJf exonuclease-assisted fluorescent self-assembly aptasensor for supersensitive detection of pesticides in food

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence quenching induced by exonuclease, specific target recognition by an aptamer, and host–guest recognition were artfully integrated in this study, and a new nanoscale fluorescent aptasensor for supersensitive recognition of acetamiprid was proposed. In the absence of acetamiprid, the aptamer hybridized with complementary DNA at 3′end-marked ferrocene (Fc) (cDNA-Fc) to form a double-stranded DNA. Fluorescence intensity was nearly unchanged after RecJf exonuclease was added. After the addition of acetamiprid, the aptamer preferentially bound with it to form a new compound; hence, the aptamer could not bind with cDNA-Fc. The single-stranded aptamer and cDNA-Fc were cut off after RecJf exonuclease was added, and the Fc released via host–guest recognition rapidly entered the cavity of cyclodextrin and initiated photoinduced electron transfer. Consequently, fluorescence intensity decreased, and acetamiprid was sensitively detected. The method can be extensively applied to detect various pesticides or additives in agriculture and forestry.

ce:section.ce:para: Acetamiprid, a novel chloride nicotine pesticide, has a contact-type broad spectrum that depends on the neural system attack mechanism [1]. Acetamiprdid is widely used to prevent or treat plant diseases and insect pests in modern agriculture because it does not develop cross-resistance with commonly used insecticides [2]. However, excessive use of insecticides leads to surplus pesticide residues in crops, vegetables, fruits, soils, and environmental water and may threaten human health and the ecosystem [3,4]. Extensive presence of acetamiprid also damages the peripheral blood lymphocytes of humans and results in DNA injury [5]. Acetamiprid is highly toxic to honeybees and hornets and can cause honeycomb disturbance, which leads to honeycomb disorganization [6]. Given these latent hazards, establishing fast and sensitive acetamiprid detection methods is crucial for agricultural safety and environmental monitoring [7]. Acetamiprid can be detected by colorimetry [8], electrochemistry [9], gas chromatography [10], high-performance liquid chromatography [11], liquid chromatography tandem–mass spectrometry [12], surface-reinforced Raman scattering [13], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [14], and liquid-phase microextraction [15]. However, these methods rely on expensive and complicated preprocessing and well-trained technicians. Thus, developing simple, fast, sensitive fluorescence biosensors for qualitative and quantitative acetamiprid detection in agricultural and food products and the environment is vital for the safeguard of the eco-environment and human health.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) with a diameter <10 nm are optically active, nontoxic and biocompatible and thus are extensively used in optical biological sensors [16,17]. N-doping improves the fluorescent quantum yields of CDs to some extent so that CDs can be effectively applied in optical sensors [18,19]. However, CDs cannot specifically select targets. Surface-functionalized CDs with specific recognizers are usually utilized to specifically identify targets.

Host–guest recognition is widely used in analytical chemistry and bioanalysis because of its high selectivity and stability [20]. β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), consisting of a hydrophobic central cavity and a hydrophilic external layer, is a typical supramolecular host that can combine with multiple guest molecules without any extra catalyst to form multifunctional self-assembly structures [21]. β-CD-functionalized QDs (β-CD-QDs) can form a nanostructured supramolecular self-assembly and have been utilized in photochemical and electrochemical sensors [22,23]. Ferrocene (Fc) is a multi-electron-orbit organic transition metal compound that is commonly used as an excited-state quencher in the photochemical field [24]. The cavity of β-CD can include Fc to form a 1:1 stable inclusion compound [25], and β-CD as a bridge can draw CDs and Fc close, thus promoting photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [20]. Afterward, the fluorescence of the β-CD-CD nanoprobe is quenched.

Aptamers, which are inexpensive, easy to modify, highly stable, and capable of super-specific binding with target molecules, are widely used to recognize proteins, toxins, and organic micromolecules [26–29]. Aptamers have been combined with sensitive and mature optical techniques and applied in aptamer biosensing technology [30,31]. RecJf exonuclease is specifically used in single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) and can catalytically remove monophosphate DNA along the 5'→3′ route, degrading ssDNA in the compound and even single-strand aptamers that bind with the target; however, double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) is unaffected [32]. Enzyme-assisted realization of signal variation makes bioluminescent sensors highly promising [33].

Inspired by these analyses, we used mono (6-amido-6-deoxy)-β- cyclodextrin to functionalize N-doped CDs and synthesized a novel fluorescent nanoprobe that has the fluorescent characteristics of CDs and the specificity of β-CD host–guest recognition. Maximizing the high sensitivity and specificity of aptamer–target binding and the host–guest recognition characteristics of Fc and β-CD, we built an exonuclease-assisted fluorescent nanoprobe that can be conveniently used to detect acetamiprid sensitively and specifically.

Acetamiprid (98%) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotech Co., Ltd. (China). Mono-(6-amino-6-deoxy)-β-cyclodextrin (97%) was provided by Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. (China). The aptamer of acetamiprid (ssDNA: 5′-TGT AAT TTG TCT GCA GCG GTT CTT GAT CGC TGA CAC CAT ATT ATG AAG A-3′) [34]and the complementary strand oligonucleotide (cDNA-Fc: 5’ -TCT TCA TAA TAT GGT GTC AGC GAT CAA GAA CCG CTG CAG ACA AAT TACA-Fc-3′) were produced and purified in Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). RecJf exonuclease and 10 × NE Buffer 2 were made of 50 mM NaCl, 10 mM MgCl2, 10 mM Tris-HCl and 1 nM DTT (pH 7.9, 25 °C) (Beijing Baiaolaibo, China). All other materials were analytically pure and diluted in 18.2 MΩ ultrapure water.

Characterizations were conducted on a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM; FEI, US, accelerated at 200 kV), an LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, US), a Nicolet 380 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) meter, a Gel Doc XR device (BIO-RAD, US), a Cary 300 UV–vis instrument (Varian, US), and a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, US) for chemical component analysis.

High-fluorescence N-doped CDs were synthesized using a previously reported method [35] with modification. Specifically, ethylenediamine (1.0 mL) and anhydrous citric acid (2.0 g) were fully dissolved in 40 mL of ultrapure water and removed to a stainless-steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluorethylene (100 mL). The autoclave was hydrothermally processed at 150 °C for 2 h, naturally cooled, purified via dialysis (molecular weight cutoff = 3500 Da) for 48 h, and freeze dried to form brown solids.

The β-CD-CD fluorescent nanoprobe was synthesized using an existing method [36] with modification. In brief, under the action of the coupling agent EDC HCl (43 mg) and NHS (51 mg) (for 1 h of carboxyl activation), mono-(6-amino-6-deoxy)-β-cyclodextrin (200 mg) containing an amino functional group was modified via an amido bond (-CO-NH-) onto the highly water-soluble CDs enriched with a carboxyl functional group (30 mL, 0.5 mg mL−1) and stirred completely for 12 h to form a novel nanoprobe (β-CD-CDs). After 24 h of dialysis (molecular weight cutoff = 3500 Da), the unreacted substances and other by-products were removed, and the resulting solution was filtered via a 0.22 μM film, freeze-dried, and stored.

Prior to use, diverse oligonucleotides were heated at 95 °C for 5 min and then cooled to ambient temperature. During this procedure, we ensured that the aptamer and cDNA-Fc adapted to their inherent conformations to facilitate the subsequent experiments. Acetamiprid with a specific concentration and cDNA-Fc (4 μM) were successively added to a mixed solution containing ssDNA (4 μM) and cultured at 37 °C for 35 min to form an aptamer–acetamiprid compound. Afterward, 30 U RecJf exonuclease was added and diluted with a 1 × NE Buffer 2–50 μL, followed by 60 min of reaction at 37 °C, to ensure that ssDNA was fully degraded by the RecJf exonuclease. Then, the enzyme was inactivated via heat treatment at 65 °C for 20 min, naturally cooled, and subsequently stored at 4 °C.

Exactly 1.4 g of powder was added to 70 mL of 1 × TBE buffer (40 mM Tris-acetic acid, 1 mM, pH 8.0) and subsequently blended under microwave heating to form 2% agarose gels. After cooling, the solution was added with 7 μL of GeneGreen and blended under slight rotation. The resulting solution was transferred to a gel tray, inserted with combs, cooled, and stored. Afterward, 7 μL of the aptamer, aptamer + RecJf exonuclease, aptamer + target, aptamer + target + RecJf exonuclease, cDNA-Fc, cDNA-Fc + RecJf exonuclease, dsDNA, and dsDNA + RecJf exonuclease with or without acetamiprid were separately added with 3 μL of a bromophenol blue solution, fully mixed, and tested via electrophoresis at 120 V for 70 min. Electrophoregrams were recorded on a Gel Doc XR system (Bio-Rad, USA).

Different oligonucleotides were processed at 95 °C for 5 min to guarantee their inherent conformations then slowly cooled to 37 °C and stored for further use. A specific acetamiprid solution (5 μL each; 0, 5, 10, 50 nM; or 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1, 1.2 μM) and 2 μL of ssDNA (4 μM) were successively added to 2 μL of a cDNA-Fc solution (4 μM) then incubated in 1 × NE Buffer 2 at 37 °C for 50 min to ensure that the acetamiprid and ssDNA were fully mixed. After the free ssDNA and cDNA-Fc were fully hybridized to form dsDNA, the mixed solution was added with 2 μL of RecJf and diluted with 1 × NE Buffer 2–50 μL. To allow the reactions to proceed completely, we incubated the mixed solutions at 37 °C for 60 min and heated the systems to 65 °C, followed by 20 min of RecJf inactivation. Afterward, the RecJf-inactivated mixed solution was added with β-CD-CDs (20 μg mL−1). The resulting solution was allowed to react at ambient temperature for 20 min after complete mixing and dilution to 5 mL. Then, the fluorescence intensity at the optimal exciting and emitting peaks of 350 and 445 nm, respectively, was measured with the fluorescence device, with the corresponding slit widths of 5 and 10 nm, respectively. To prevent background impacts, we used ΔF/F0 instead of fluorescence intensity when assessing the optimal detection conditions of the aptasensor. ΔF was calculated as F0–F, where F0 and F were the values detected without and with acetamiprid, respectively.

Spiked recovery experiments with honey or with orange juice were carried out to assess the practicability of the developed nanoscale fluorescent aptasensor. Honey and oranges were bought from a supermarket in Linfen, China. Exactly 5 g of honey dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water was filtered using 0.22 μM organic filtrate membranes and diluted 100 times before the subsequent experiments. The fresh oranges were ground into homogenate, which was filtered via a quadrilayer gauze. After 15 min of centrifugation at 5000 rpm, the supernatant of the raw juice was filtered through 0.22 μM organic membranes. The resulting solution was diluted 100 times and stored for further use.

To validate the accuracy and feasibility of our method, we analyzed these samples using liquid chromatography-mass spectrography (LC-MS). The samples were processed as reported before [37] with some modification. In brief, fresh oranges were ground into homogenate, from which 10 g was collected and put into a centrifuge tube (50 mL). After addition of 15 mL of an extractive solvent (acetonitrile/acetic acid = 99/1), the tube was fully vibrated and then added with CH3COONa (1 g) and MgSO4 (4 g), followed by vibration. After that, the resulting mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. Then 5 mL of the supernatant was transferred to a 10 mL centrifuge tube and added with MgSO4 (0.9 g), primary secondary amine (PSA, 0.3 g) and graphitized carbon black (GCB, 0.3 g), followed by centrifugation. Finally, 1 mL of the new supernatant was collected, filtrated through a 0.22 μM PTFE membrane and characterized by LC-MS. As for preparation of honey samples, firstly 5 g of honey dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water was removed to a centrifuge tube (50 mL), processed in the same way as the orange samples, and detected via LC-MS.

The β-CD-functionalized N-CDs were observed via TEM, UV–vis spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy, and XPS (See in Supplementary Material Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). The β-CD-CDs possessed the host–guest recognizing ability of β-CD and the optical properties of CDs, because N-CDs were functionalized via -CO-NH- with β-CD. β-CD can bind with Fc via guest-host matching to form a 1:1 inclusion compound [25,38], and when Fc enters the cavity of the β-CD-CDs, the formation of the inclusion compound is accompanied with the opened Fc-CDs PET. Furthermore, the presence of β-CD creates a convenient and direct bridge for the occurrence of PET [20].

In the developed system, the aptamer of acetamiprid (ssDNA) hybridized with cDNA with 3′-end-modified Fc (cDNA-Fc) to form dsDNA when the target acetamiprid was absent. Given that RecJf exonuclease could not degrade it, dsDNA after addition could not get into the cavity of the β-CD-CD nanoprobe (Fc could not enter the cavity) due to the steric hindrance. Hence, the fluorescence intensity in the system was nearly unchanged. When acetamiprid and cDNA-Fc coexisted, acetamiprid was highly affinitive with its aptamer and preferentially bonded to form an aptamer–acetamiprid compound rather than forming the aptamer–cDNA-Fc double-stranded structure. The ssDNA and cDNA-Fc in the aptamer–acetamiprid compound were degraded into single deoxynucleotides when RecJf exonuclease was added, so acetamiprid and the Fc modified on the complementary strand were released. After the β-CD-CD nanoprobe was added, Fc and the nanoprobe underwent host–guest recognition and successively got into the hole of β-CD, which initiated the CDs-Fc PET, thus decreasing the fluorescence (Fig. 1 ). Furthermore, the released acetamiprid could be recovered and re-bind with the aptamer, thereby initiating potential exonuclease-assisted target recycling.

The introduction of the aptamer into the reaction system ensured a highly sensitive and specific recognition of the target. The involvement of RecJf exonuclease in the system considerably decreased the interference from background signals and further increased the reaction sensitivity.

To confirm the feasibility of the developed strategy further, we conducted RecJf exonuclease-induced target recovery experiments through agarose gel electrophoresis (Fig. 2 ). After the addition of RecJf exonuclease, the samples were cultured in nine swim lanes at 37 °C for 60 min, and heated at 65 °C for 20 min to inactivate the enzyme that generated the digestion products of this exonuclease. Lanes 1 (aptamer), 3 (aptamer–acetamiprid compound), and 5 (cDNA-Fc) each had a single-stranded DNA and were thus manifested as bright bands and had similar migration rates. With the presence of RecJf exonuclease, the single-stranded DNAs were fragmented and taken out of the gels; thus, no bands were observed on lanes 2, 4, or 6. The absence of bands on lane 4 indicates that RecJf exonuclease could also degrade the ssDNA of the aptamer–acetamiprid compound. Given that RecJf exonuclease could not hydrolyze dsDNA, we observed evident bands and lower migration rates than ssDNA on lane 7 containing dsDNA and lane 8 containing dsDNA + Recjf exonuclease. On lane 9, the addition of acetamiprid successfully triggered a cyclic reaction and preferentially formed an aptamer–acetamiprid compound. Thus, ssDNA and cDNA-Fc were found in the system. After RecJf exonuclease was added, ssDNA and cDNA-Fc were hydrolyzed, and no bands were found on lane 9. These results imply that RecJf exonuclease could not degrade dsDNA and could only decompose ssDNA and cDNA-Fc. After the aptamer–acetamiprid compound was formed, the aptamer was degraded. This result means that the released acetamiprid was formed from the degradation of the aptamer–acetamiprid compound by RecJf exonuclease.

To validate the targeted recovery fluorescence quenching effect induced by RecJf exonuclease, we analyzed the feasibility of the aptasensor potential recycling system. Fig. 3 displays the fluorescence signals without and with acetamiprid. When acetamiprid was not added, ssDNA and cDNA-Fc hybridized to form dsDNA, which hindered the reaction with RecJf exonuclease. Thus, the fluorescence variation in the system can be ignored (curve b). The major reason was that the Fc modifying dsDNA could not be captured by the cavity of CD because of steric hindrance, and when no bridge was created, Fc could not diffuse freely to the surface of CDs, thus quenching the fluorescence slightly. When only acetamiprid but no RecJf exonuclease was added, cyclodextrin could also undergo host–guest recognition with ssDNA, which enhanced the fluorescence intensity through sensitization (curve c) [39]. When acetamiprid and RecJf exonuclease coexisted, an aptamer–acetamiprid compound and single-stranded cDNA-Fc were formed due to the specific binding between acetamiprid and ssDNA. The 5'→3′ catalytic removal of RecJf exonuclease from monophosphate deoxynucleotide ensured that the ssDNA after the targeted binding between single-stranded cDNA-Fc and the target could be fully degraded into monodeoxynucleotide. Given that single bases nearly could not cause any change to the fluorescence intensity [40], the fluorescence enhancement that may be induced by ssDNA complexation in the cavity of cyclodextrin could be avoided. Thus, after Fc was released, the free Fc could be rapidly captured by the cavity of cyclodextrin, which quenched the fluorescence (curve d). Afterward, the released target participated in the next reaction cycle, producing increased fluorescent signal quenchers.

These phenomena suggest that the participation of RecJf exonuclease led to the evident variation in fluorescence intensity. In the entire reaction system, cyclodextrin captured Fc and rapidly initiated PET between Fc and CDs.

To enhance the detection sensitivity of the aptasensor toward acetamiprid, we optimized all of the parameters involved in aptasensor establishment and acetamiprid detection. Acetamiprid detection was considerably affected by cDNA-Fc concentration, RecJf exonuclease concentration and degradation time, and Fc capture time of cyclodextrin.

cDNA-Fc concentration is critical for fluoroscopic detection because low-concentration cDNA-Fc may lead to insufficient support for digestion cycles, whereas excessive cDNA-Fc may result in overly high background signals. The dosage of cDNA-Fc within 0.5–5 μM was assessed in this study. At the level of 4 μM, the resulting Fc could be captured by β-CD-CDs to the largest extent or fluorescence intensity was reduced to the lowest level (Fig. 4 A). When the concentration rose further, fluorescence began to increase probably because excess cDNA-Fc may intensify steric hindrance and weaken the Fc capturing ability of β-CD-CDs.

The concentration and degradation time of RecJf exonuclease are also important to the performance of the aptasensor. When the concentration of RecJf exonuclease was too low, it could not fully hydrolyze the cDNA-Fc and aptamer in the system. Thus, the single-stranded DNA in the system was seized by β-CD-CDs, and fluorescence was intensified. Consequently, 1 μM acetamiprid was added for 50 min of culture at 37 °C to ensure that the aptamer and acetamiprid could bind completely (Fig. S3). When the dosage of RecJf exonuclease increased, ΔF/F0 reached a turning point at 24U. The continued decrease in fluorescence intensity verified that the reactions proceeded completely when the dosage of RecJf exonuclease reached 24U. However, to ensure the complete hydrolysis of ssDNA in the system, we increased the dosage of RecJf exonuclease to 30U (Fig. 4B). The optimal dosage of RecJf exonuclease in the subsequent experiments was 30U.

The lack of enzyme cleavage time can also result in the formation of ssDNA. The effect of enzyme cleavage time within 10–90 min was studied (Fig. 4C). The enzyme cleavage reaction occurred within the first 60 min; after that, ΔF/F0 was unchanged. Thus, the optimal reaction time of enzyme cleavage was considered to be 60 min.

The time of free Fc capture by the cavity of cyclodextrin is another critical factor to enhance the sensitivity of the aptasensor. We studied the fluorescence intensity within the capture time of 5–50 min. During the incubation with β-CD-CDs (20 μg mL−1), the quenching effect was optimized after 35 min and was unchanged within the next 15 min (Fig. 4D). Thus, the optimal quenching time was 35 min.
Enzymes under high alkalinity or acidity may be inactivated. Thus, the optimum pH of enzymes was controlled at 7.9.

The fluorescence aptasensor, which integrates β-CD-based host–guest recognition and RecJf exonuclease-assisted fluorescence quenching, was used to detect acetamiprid quantitatively. The fluorescence gradually decreased with the increase in acetamiprid concentration (5, 10, 50 nM; 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1, 1.2 μM) in the aptasensor under optimal experimental conditions (Fig. 5 ). As expected, when the acetamiprid concentration increased, additional aptamer–acetamiprid compounds were formed, and when RecJf exonuclease was added, the released Fc was captured by β-CD, which led to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. ΔF/F0 was plotted against the logarithmic value of acetamiprid concentration. The two were linearly related within a certain range (5 nM–1.2 μM): ΔF/F0 = 0.0863 lg CAcetamiprid − 0.1111 (R2 = 0.995). According to 3s/k (s is the relative standard deviation [RSD] of 11 blank signals without acetamiprid; k is the linear-curve slope), the limit of detection (LOD) was computed to be 3 nM (RSD = ±3.9).

Compared with other acetamiprid detection methods, our method has satisfactory sensitivity and a linear range (Tab. S1). The LOD of our method is significantly lower than that of several fluorescence methods [41], electrochemical impedance spectroscopy [1], differential pulse voltammetry [4], colorimetry [5], and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [42], but it still cannot surpass the LOD of electrochemistry methods [43] or chemiluminescence [44]. Nevertheless, our method has a broad linear range and avoids complicated sample pretreatment. We used environmentally-friendly light sources, and adopted nontoxic carbon nanomaterials that can be easily prepared. These features reduce costs and are favorable for food security and eco-environment conservation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, interference, and stability of acetamiprid detection
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity and interference of the new aptasensor were tested by comparing nine coexisting insecticides that had similar functions or structures as acetamiprid; these nine insecticides were imidacloprid, glufosinate ammonium, beta cypermethrin, metalaxyl, isocarbophos, phoxim, chlorpyrifos, atrazine, and 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid. Fluorescence intensity was not significantly reduced (Fig. 6 ) even under the same detection conditions and when the concentration of any of the nine insecticides was twice that of acetamiprid (500 nM). The reason was that the specific binding between the aptamer and acetamiprid ensured the high selectivity of the system.

The interference experiments (Fig. 6) showed that when 500 nM acetamiprid and a twofold concentration of any of the nine substances coexisted, the fluorescence intensity of the aptasensor was not significantly changed, indicating that the aptasensor did not affect acetamiprid detection when these substances and acetamiprid coexisted.
These results imply that the new aptasensor is highly specific to acetamiprid and has high anti-interference ability.

A β-CD-CD fluorescent probe (20 μg mL−1) was synthesized under excitation at 350 nm and emission at 445 nm. The fluorescent signals of the nanoprobe were detected on alternate days within five weeks, and the remaining probe systems were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. The fluorescence signal value of the probe declined by 4.19% (RSD ≤ ±2.88%, n = 3) (Fig. S4), and the probe emitted bright light-blue light under UV illumination. This result fully verifies the high stability of the new β-CD-CD fluorescent probe.

Acetamiprid with a contact-type broad spectrum is applied to fruits and vegetables, and it is highly toxic to honeybees. We selected honey and orange juice to test the applicability of the new aptasensor further. To validate the feasibility of the fluorescence aptasensor, we detected the same samples via LC-MS. Resulted showed that neither fluorescence aptasensor nor LC-MS detected residual acetamiprid in these original samples. Thus, the honey samples and orange juice samples were separately added with 5, 10 or 50 nM acetamiprid. Then acetamiprid in the polluted samples was detected in the same way. The acetamiprid recovery rates were within 94.9%–104.2% in the honey solutions and within 96.8%–105.5% in the orange juice solutions. All RSDs were less than 4.01% (Tab. S2). The acetamiprid concentrations detected by the fluorescence aptasensor were consistent with those by LC-MS, indicating that the new aptasensor can detect acetamiprid in actual food samples. The high accuracy and reproducibility also confirm that this probe has a potential for application in the food security field.

Pesticide residues are a major concern in food security and environmental pollution. Thus, establishing safe, reliable, efficient methods for qualitative and qualitative analyses of residual pesticides is urgent. We developed a nanoscale fluorescent aptasensor based on the specific acetamiprid recognizing ability of the aptamer via fluorescence quenching due to PET between β-CD-CDs and Fc and by fully utilizing the auxiliary effect of RecJf exonuclease. Fluorescence intensity was linearly related with acetamiprid concentration within 5 nM−1.2 μM (R2 = 0.995), with LOD of 3 nM. Although the presented method does not have the lowest LOD among the compared approaches, it outperforms the others in terms of selectivity, sensitivity, safety, and linear range. Thus, it can be potentially applied in food security, environmental safety, and other fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing ﬁnancial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to inﬂuence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2020.117469.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective electrochemical impedance spectroscopy-based aptasensor for sensitive detection of acetamiprid
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Fan, G. Zhao, H. Shi, M. Liu, Z. Li, A highly selective electrochemical impedance spectroscopy-based aptasensor for sensitive detection of acetamiprid, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 43(2013) 12-18.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cross-resistance, the stability of acetamiprid resistance and its effect on the biological parameters of cotton mealybug, Phenacoccus solenopsis (Homoptera: pseudococcidae), in Pakistan
Pest Manag. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.B. Afzal, S.A. Shad, N. Abbas, M. Ayyaz, W.B. Walker, Cross-resistance, the stability of acetamiprid resistance and its effect on the biological parameters of cotton mealybug, Phenacoccus solenopsis (Homoptera: Pseudococcidae), in Pakistan, Pest management science, 71(2015) 151-158.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assessment of potential (inhalation and dermal) and actual exposure to acetamiprid by greenhouse applicators using liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
Journal of chromatography B, Analytical technologies in the biomedical and life sciences

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Marin, J.L. Martinez Vidal, F.J. Egea Gonzalez, A. Garrido Frenich, C.R. Glass, M.
